visible_observations: A hairless pale-gray humanoid crouches on long clawed hands and feet, with a smooth elongated head, two large black eye cavities, no visible mouth, corded neck and chest anatomy, and sparse etched symbols.
Numerous flat black blade-like tendrils emerge behind the torso and curl around the figure in large hooked arcs.
The blank face, low many-point stance, and radiating hooked appendages create a specific alien silhouette without clothing or carried equipment.
defects: The head tilt, lowered body, and still open-handed crouch can read as wary observation or vulnerability rather than an imminent attack.
The tendrils form a near-even decorative halo around the body and do not visibly contact, target, or manipulate anything.
The smooth gray skin, black almond eye voids, and narrow humanoid proportions also resemble a familiar generic gray-alien or masked mannequin base.
Fine surface etchings are isolated decorative marks and do not visibly alter the body or explain the tendril anatomy.
uncertainties: The image does not establish whether the black appendages are attached anatomy, shadows, blades, or an external effect.
The empty eye cavities provide head direction but no visible pupil focus, and the absent mouth removes most conventional intent cues.
The creature's scale and method of attack remain unresolved in the isolated pose.

visible_observations: Three black candidates show a crouched figure encircled by many radiating hooked tendrils, a tall upright figure enclosed by long parallel appendages, and a low sprawling runner under one enormous looping appendage.
The candidates preserve a small smooth head and long humanoid limbs while changing posture, appendage count, and the spatial relationship between body and tendrils.
defects: The left candidate's tendrils create a pinwheel or flower-like frame that can overpower the creature's hostile body direction.
The center candidate is highly symmetrical and closed, with the appendages reading as a cocoon or decorative enclosure rather than active limbs.
The right candidate reduces the appendage system to one dominant loop, making its relationship to the multi-tendril versions unclear.
uncertainties: The image provides no A, B, C labels or explicit selected-candidate marker.
Solid black presentation cannot show whether the apparent appendage-count changes are anatomical alternatives or overlapping views of one structure.

visible_observations: The smooth elongated head angles downward and toward the viewer, with two deep black almond-shaped eye cavities, a central seam or scar line, and no visible mouth, brows, nose movement, or ears.
Corded anatomy runs from the jaw and neck into the shoulder and chest, while black tendrils frame the head from behind.
defects: The featureless eye voids and absent mouth make attention broadly directional but prevent a specific emotional or interpersonal read.
The slight head tilt can suggest curiosity, sadness, caution, or predation with equal plausibility.
The smooth mask-like face remains visually detached from the hooked tendrils because no strain, attachment response, or facial consequence is visible.
uncertainties: Unaided viewing cannot determine what the creature wants from the viewer, whether it is afraid, or whether it recognizes the viewer at all.
It is unclear whether the face is skin, a mask, a shell, or scarred anatomy.

visible_observations: At native size, the pale rounded head, low four-point body, long forearms, and several large black curling appendages remain visible.
The pale center against the dark tendril ring creates a readable spider-like or alien radial shape.
defects: The eye cavities, body etchings, claws, chest anatomy, and individual tendril attachments collapse at this size.
Several black tendrils merge into the background and into one another, reducing their count and direction.
The surviving radial outline reads more clearly as a generic many-limbed creature than as a specific facial or behavioral identity.
uncertainties: No neighboring unit icons, gameplay background, selection frame, or motion state are shown, so in-context recognition and hostility cannot be determined.
